patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "Hi there, I'm Doctor Smith from Babylon."] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>
[Clinician asked: "Hi. Can you just confirm your name, date of birth and your address, please?"] Yes. <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Italy. And,
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> I see, sounds a bit <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> . Um. Are you in a private place so you can have a consultation today?"] Sorry?
[Clinician asked: "Are you in a private place so you can have a consultation today?"] Uh, yes.
[Clinician asked: "What can I do for you?"] So, I have had a  pain in my lower tummy the last two days. I feel kind of hot and sweaty.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> OK. And any pain like this in the past?"] Uh, not yet, no.
[Clinician asked: "OK, can you just describe to me whereabouts it is?"] Um, so, I don't, what, sorry?
[Clinician asked: "Can you describe to me whereabouts the pain is? Is it, is it."] So it's my lower tummy.
[Clinician asked: "Yeah. Is it in the middle or to one side?"] Uh, not, um, with a needle.
[Clinician asked: "In the middle, OK. And any other symptoms along that, with that? Have you had any nausea or any vomiting?"] Uh, no. I'm just a bit nausea but no vomiting.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Any change in your bowel habit recently, or any diarrhoea? Constipation?"] Uh, I had been constipated in, like, in the last, um, last week, but now it's fine. And, yeah, like
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> to the toilet?"] Sorry?
[Clinician asked: "When did you last go to the toilet?"] Uh, like two hours ago.
[Clinician asked: "And was it normal?"] Yeah.
[Clinician asked: "Any blood in the stool?"] Um, so there was some, like, slight, uh, blood in the urine, but, like,  it was a little bit pink but.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And has this ever happened in the past before?"] Um, no.
[Clinician asked: "Right. Any pain when you pass water?"] Uh, no, that's fine.
[Clinician asked: "Are you having to go more often than normal?"] Uh, yeah, a little bit, but I wasn't drinking a lot in these days.
[Clinician asked: "Are you more thirsty than normal?"] Uh, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "And you said you felt hot and cold. Have you measured your temperature?"] Uh, yes, I measured it but it's fine, like, around thirty seven.
[Clinician asked: "What was it? Thirty seven. Have you measured it at any other time when it's been raised?"] Yeah. Sorry?
[Clinician asked: "Have you measured it at any other time when it's been raised?"] Um, no, no, I just measured, like, one hour ago.

[Clinician asked: "OK, that's fine. And, are you getting any pain higher up in your stomach? Any acid coming up into your throat or anything like that?"] No, no,  but like just a bit of nausea but not vomiting.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And any other discharge below?"] Uh, no.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Um, and have you been abroad recently?"] Um, no, no.
[Clinician asked: "No. And no similar problems to this before?"] No.
[Clinician asked: "Have you had any other medical problems in the past?"] Um, no, no, nothing sore. I mean, depends what, like, normal stuff like cold, flu.
[Clinician asked: "OK, nothing  significant. Any allergies to medications?"] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Uh, yes, amoxycillin.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Do you take any regular medications?"] Uh, no.
[Clinician asked: "Any over the counter medications?"] No. I have had the just, the, uh, internal  uh, contraceptive implant, but like, for one year, now, no more.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Um, and do you have regular periods?"] Uh, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "Any bleeding in between periods or after intercourse?"] Um, no, no, no.
[Clinician asked: "OK."] Just like <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>
[Clinician asked: "Right. Um, and you haven't felt shivery or sweaty? You said you had felt a bit sweaty."] Yeah. <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> , yeah.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And any, any back pain or strong smell to the urine?"] Uh, no back pain. No, I typically don't suffer from back pain, so, no.

[Clinician asked: "Right, OK. Anyone in your family had any medical problems before?"] Uh, so,  actually my mom had the breast cancer. Uh, but, again, not so important. I mean, just, like, the beginning it was , uh, cured in time.
[Clinician asked: "OK. OK. And who do you live with at home?"] Um, sorry?
[Clinician asked: "Who do you live with at home?"] Uh, with my parents.
[Clinician asked: "And, do you smoke  or drink alcohol regularly?"] Uh, yes.
[Clinician asked: "How much in a normal week?"] Uh, so, I don't smoke. I drink, I <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> . Four times per week.
[Clinician asked: "And how much?"] Uh, yeah, a beer  like, per time, let's say. <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> .
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> What per time?"] Uh, let's say a beer.
[Clinician asked: "OK."] Four times a week on average, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "Right, OK. Um, so, it, it may be possible that you might be having an infection in the urine."] Uh-huh.
[Clinician asked: "If you feel like you're, you're going, you're having a little bit of difficulty when you're passing urine."] Yeah.

[Clinician asked: "Um, it might be worth taking a three day course of antibiotics. To clear that. Um, but, if the pain is getting worse, or you have a high temperature or back pain or develop any new symptoms, we feel like it's not getting better, then, um, we should speak to you again, because we might need to see yo"] Uh-huh. Uh-huh, OK.
[Clinician asked: "Um, have you allocated a pharmacy with Babylon?"] Uh, yes.
[Clinician asked: "OK. So, are you happy to take a course of antibiotics, if I send it through to the pharmacy?"] Uh, yeah, that's fine. If it helps, yeah. OK.
[Clinician asked: "OK, so I can do that for you. Start taking them as soon as you get them, it's just twice a day for three days. Um, if your pain's getting worse,"] OK.
[Clinician asked: "um, or you've got new symptoms, just contact us straightaway, or if you're getting a recurrence of the same problem again."] Uh-huh, OK. Sure. OK.
[Clinician asked: "OK? Alright then, have you got any other questions?"] Uh, no, that's fine. Yeah. Thank you. Thank you. Bye.
gold_note:
PC: Abdo pain
HPC: 2/7 hx lower abdominal pain, central location. Has noticed some blood in the urine. Has check temp - 37. No back pain. Feels sweaty. More thirsty than normal, drinking well. No dysuria/freq. Constipated last week, now BO normally. No blood in stool. Last BO 2 hrs ago. No N&V, no dyspepsia. 

PMH : Nil 
DH: Nil. Allergic to amoxicillin.
FMH: Mum had breast cancer, now in remission. 
SH: Lives with parents. Non smoker. No drugs. Drinks 1 beer x 4/week.

Imp: ? UTI.
Plan: Advised 3/7 course Abx. Will pick up from pharmacy. To start asap. If develops temperature/persistent/worsening pain to call back or if recurrent similar sx.

gold_sections.HIGHLIGHTS:
lower abdominal pain
blood in the urine
temp - 37
No back pain
thirsty

naive.response_text:
I'm really sorry to hear you're feeling this way. It sounds like you're experiencing some concerning symptoms, and I understand how unsettling that can be. Based on what you've described, it seems like you might have a urinary tract infection (UTI), which is common and treatable. The pain and slight blood in your urine are typical signs of a UTI, especially since you don't have a history of such issues.

I recommend starting a three-day course of antibiotics as prescribed, which should help alleviate your symptoms. Make sure to take the medication exactly as directed. If your pain worsens, you develop a high fever, or you experience back pain, please contact us immediately, as these could indicate a more serious infection.

In the meantime, stay hydrated and monitor your symptoms. If you have any concerns or questions about the treatment, don't hesitate to reach out. I'm here to support you, and I'll make sure to address any worries you might have. Take care, and I hope you feel better soon.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns with me. It sounds like you're feeling uncomfortable with the lower tummy pain and the presence of blood in your urine, and I can understand why that would be worrying. It's important that we address these symptoms to find a solution.

Based on what you've described, a few possibilities come to mind. The most common concern is a urinary tract infection (UTI), which can cause pain, blood in the urine, and a slight fever. UTIs are quite common and often respond well to antibiotics [PMID ?]. Another consideration is kidney stones, which can cause similar symptoms, especially if the pain is severe or one-sided. If your symptoms worsen, such as if you develop back pain or nausea, it would be important to seek further evaluation [PMID ?].

Given the current situation, I recommend starting a three-day course of antibiotics to treat a potential UTI. These should be taken twice daily as prescribed. If your symptoms don't improve or if they get worse, please reach out to us immediately. We'll also schedule a follow-up in two weeks to ensure everything is improving.

Before you go, could you summarize the plan in your own words? That way, I can make sure you understand the next steps. We'll be in touch in two weeks to check in.
